$comment: Cross-SDK canonicalisation test vectors for ADR-0009. Each vector under canonicalization_vectors asserts that SDK.canonicalize(input) produces canonical byte-for-byte. Each vector under receipt_hash_vectors asserts that SDK.hashReceipt(receipt) produces expectedHash; these vectors exercise the Rule 2 null-normalisation in addition to the raw canonicaliser. NOTE: the `receipt` field on some vectors intentionally carries schema-INVALID caller input (e.g. optional fields set to null) in order to exercise the SDK's pre-serialization normalisation sweep; such vectors are marked `preNormalizationInput: true`. They are not conformant receipts and must never be emitted as-is. All strings are UTF-8.

canonicalization_vectors.name: empty_object
canonicalization_vectors.description: Empty object serialises to {}.

canonicalization_vectors.name: empty_array
canonicalization_vectors.description: Empty array serialises to [].

canonicalization_vectors.name: sort_ascii
canonicalization_vectors.description: ASCII key order — baseline.

canonicalization_vectors.name: sort_ascii_nested
canonicalization_vectors.description: Recursive key sort at all depths.

canonicalization_vectors.name: sort_bmp_at_ascii_boundary
canonicalization_vectors.description: U+007F (DEL) vs U+0080 (C1 control) — both below the UTF-16-LE byte-order bug threshold; catches the Go UTF-8-byte-sort bug (#82) since the UTF-8 encoding of U+0080 is 0xC2 0x80 (two bytes, leading 0xC2), whereas 0x7F is one byte (0x7F). Sort by UTF-16 code units: 0x007F < 0x0080.

canonicalization_vectors.name: sort_bmp_above_00ff
canonicalization_vectors.description: U+00FF (ÿ) vs U+0100 (Ā) — catches the Python UTF-16-LE byte-order bug (#86). UTF-16-LE bytes: 0xFF 0x00 vs 0x00 0x01 → byte sort puts 0x00 0x01 (Ā) first, which is WRONG. UTF-16 code-unit sort: 0x00FF < 0x0100 → ÿ first, which is correct.

canonicalization_vectors.name: sort_supplementary_plane
canonicalization_vectors.description: 🎉 (U+1F389) vs private-use U+E000. Code-point sort would put U+E000 < U+1F389 — wrong. UTF-16 sort: 🎉 encodes as surrogate pair 0xD83C 0xDF89, so the first code unit is 0xD83C which is less than 0xE000 → 🎉 sorts first.

canonicalization_vectors.name: parameters_disclosure_multikey_sort
canonicalization_vectors.description: Pins the canonical form of an action.parameters_disclosure map (ADR-0012 Phase A) with two keys. The field is a plain string→string map and flows through the same canonicaliser as every other map, so this is a key-sort vector rather than a parameters_disclosure-specific rule. Input has insertion order user→command; canonical output sorts to command→user.

canonicalization_vectors.name: number_zero
canonicalization_vectors.description: Positive zero.

canonicalization_vectors.name: number_negative_zero
canonicalization_vectors.description: -0 must serialise as 0 per RFC 8785. This vector uses JSON -0.0 (rather than -0) so standard decoders preserve IEEE 754 negative zero without requiring harness special-casing: Python json.loads, JavaScript JSON.parse, and Go json.Unmarshal into float64/interface{} all round-trip -0.0 as a float with a negative sign bit.

canonicalization_vectors.name: number_integer_positive
canonicalization_vectors.description: Integer value encoded as a number.

canonicalization_vectors.name: number_integer_negative
canonicalization_vectors.description: Negative integer.

canonicalization_vectors.name: number_simple_decimal
canonicalization_vectors.description: Exact binary fraction — same repr across all SDKs.

canonicalization_vectors.name: number_shortest_repr
canonicalization_vectors.description: 0.1 has no exact binary representation; ES6 Number.toString returns the shortest decimal that round-trips (0.1).

canonicalization_vectors.name: number_fp_addition
canonicalization_vectors.description: 0.1 + 0.2 = 0.30000000000000004 under IEEE 754 double. Tests the shortest-round-trippable rule at full precision.

canonicalization_vectors.name: number_small_exp_boundary_fixed
canonicalization_vectors.description: 1e-6 renders as fixed notation (0.000001) per ES6.

canonicalization_vectors.name: number_small_exp_boundary_exp
canonicalization_vectors.description: 1e-7 crosses the ES6 exponential threshold (exp < -6) and renders as 1e-7 — NOT 1e-07, 0.0000001, or 1E-7. Pins sign and digit formatting.

canonicalization_vectors.name: number_large_exp_boundary_fixed
canonicalization_vectors.description: 1e20 is at the boundary where ES6 still uses fixed notation (exp < 21).

canonicalization_vectors.name: number_large_exp_boundary_exp
canonicalization_vectors.description: 1e21 crosses the ES6 exponential threshold and renders as 1e+21. The + is mandatory (not just 1e21).

canonicalization_vectors.name: number_2_to_53
canonicalization_vectors.description: 2^53 = 9007199254740992 is exactly representable in IEEE 754 and appears as an integer.

canonicalization_vectors.name: string_empty
canonicalization_vectors.description: Empty string.

canonicalization_vectors.name: string_simple_ascii
canonicalization_vectors.description: Plain ASCII string.

canonicalization_vectors.name: string_escape_quote_and_backslash
canonicalization_vectors.description: Mandatory RFC 8259 escapes: \" and \\.

canonicalization_vectors.name: string_escape_control_chars
canonicalization_vectors.description: Control chars escape as per ES6 JSON.stringify: \b, \f, \n, \r, \t for the named ones; \u00XX for the rest. Pins specifically that U+0001 uses \u0001 lowercase hex.

canonicalization_vectors.name: string_no_html_escape
canonicalization_vectors.description: Chars that Go's default json.Marshal HTML-escapes (<, >, &) MUST be emitted verbatim per RFC 8785. Catches the Go HTML-escape divergence (discovered during ADR-0009 review).

canonicalization_vectors.name: string_no_u2028_escape
canonicalization_vectors.description: U+2028 LINE SEPARATOR and U+2029 PARAGRAPH SEPARATOR are valid JSON chars per RFC 8259 and RFC 8785 — emitted verbatim, NOT escaped. ES5 JSON.stringify escaped them; ES6+ does not. SDKs must track the ES6 behaviour.

canonicalization_vectors.name: string_bmp_non_ascii
canonicalization_vectors.description: Non-ASCII BMP chars are emitted verbatim (UTF-8 encoded in output), not escaped.

canonicalization_vectors.name: string_supplementary_plane
canonicalization_vectors.description: Supplementary-plane char emitted verbatim as the UTF-8 byte sequence for U+1F389 (F0 9F 8E 89).

canonicalization_vectors.name: bool_and_null
canonicalization_vectors.description: Literals.

canonicalization_vectors.name: deep_nesting
canonicalization_vectors.description: Ten-level nesting of objects and arrays, pinning that recursion works end-to-end.

canonicalization_vectors.name: parameters_hash_ascii
canonicalization_vectors.description: mcp-proxy's parameters_hash must use RFC 8785 (#118). ASCII-only baseline: SHA-256 of the canonical bytes equals a known value. Note: ASCII cannot distinguish a conformant RFC 8785 canonicaliser from Go's `json.Marshal` shortcut — both sort ASCII keys identically. This vector only asserts parity with the known canonical bytes; the `parameters_hash_supplementary_vs_bmp` vector is what actually catches the Go bug. SDKs compute canonical bytes of `input` and assert `expectedHash` = sha256(canonical).

canonicalization_vectors.name: parameters_hash_supplementary_vs_bmp
canonicalization_vectors.description: Supplementary-plane key (🎉, U+1F389) vs BMP key (U+E000). This pair genuinely diverges between UTF-8 byte sort and UTF-16 code-unit sort — it is the vector that catches Go's `json.Marshal` shortcut (#118). UTF-16 code-unit sort: 🎉's first code unit is 0xD83C < 0xE000, so 🎉 sorts first (RFC 8785 — correct). UTF-8 byte sort: 🎉 = F0 9F 8E 89 vs U+E000 = EE 80 80 — EE < F0, so U+E000 would sort first (Go's default — wrong). The canonical bytes and hash below commit to the RFC 8785 order.

receipt_hash_vectors.name: receipt_all_optional_absent
receipt_hash_vectors.description: A v0.2.1 receipt with no optional fields present. Canonical form is the minimum-size receipt; signature verifies. Serves as the baseline against which 'optional-null' vectors are compared.

receipt_hash_vectors.name: receipt_optional_null_becomes_absent
receipt_hash_vectors.description: INTENTIONALLY SCHEMA-INVALID caller input. The receipt below sets optional fields (action.trusted_timestamp, outcome.error) to null, which the tightened 0.2.1 schema forbids. It is never emitted on the wire — it only exists to exercise the SDK's pre-serialization normalisation sweep. The post-sweep canonicaliser MUST normalise null-valued optional fields to absent before hashing; the resulting canonical bytes MUST be identical to receipt_all_optional_absent. Pre-sweep Python does this (exclude_none); pre-sweep TS would include the null, producing a different hash — this vector catches that regression.

receipt_hash_vectors.name: receipt_parameters_disclosure_null_becomes_absent
receipt_hash_vectors.description: INTENTIONALLY SCHEMA-INVALID caller input. action.parameters_disclosure is set to null, which the 0.2.1 schema (and ADR-0012 Phase A) forbids. The post-sweep canonicaliser MUST normalise null-valued optional fields to absent before hashing; the resulting canonical bytes MUST be identical to receipt_all_optional_absent. Mirrors receipt_optional_null_becomes_absent for the parameters_disclosure field specifically (regression test for ADR-0012 rollout).

receipt_hash_vectors.name: receipt_required_null_preserved
receipt_hash_vectors.description: previous_receipt_hash is required-nullable; when null it MUST appear as "previous_receipt_hash":null in the canonical bytes. This vector is identical to receipt_all_optional_absent — included separately to document the required-nullable rule explicitly and pin a regression test against an SDK that accidentally drops it.

receipt_hash_vectors.name: receipt_non_ascii_resource
receipt_hash_vectors.description: credentialSubject.action.target.resource contains non-ASCII Unicode (literal UTF-8, no escaping). Exercises the key-sort rule at the receipt level — the other keys are ASCII but the SDK's canonicaliser must preserve this string verbatim and sort surrounding keys correctly.

receipt_hash_vectors.name: receipt_html_chars_in_resource
receipt_hash_vectors.description: credentialSubject.action.target.resource contains <, >, & — MUST NOT be HTML-escaped. Catches the Go json.Marshal HTML-escape bug (Rule 4 bonus).

receipt_hash_vectors.name: receipt_all_optional_present
receipt_hash_vectors.description: A v0.2.1 receipt with every currently-defined optional field populated with a non-null value. Pins the canonical form of the 'maximal' receipt. Compare against receipt_all_optional_absent to confirm each optional field contributes a unique canonical-byte sequence.

receipt_hash_vectors.name: receipt_signature_preservation_legacy_0_2_0
receipt_hash_vectors.description: The three-receipt terminalChain from cross-sdk-tests/v020_vectors.json was signed under the pre-sweep (0.2.0) canonicaliser. Running the post-sweep canonicaliser on the same unsigned bytes MUST produce identical canonical output, and the existing proof.proofValue MUST still verify. This is the signature-preservation invariant from ADR-0009 §4. Implementation: this vector reuses the v020 fixtures by reference; the test harness loads both files and asserts round-trip equality.
receipt_hash_vectors.invariant: post-sweep canonicalize(unsigned(r)) == pre-sweep canonical bytes; verifySignature(r) == true

notes_for_implementers: All strings in this file are UTF-8. JSON unicode escapes (\uXXXX) are equivalent to their literal characters after parsing — SDK test harnesses should parse the file with any standard JSON parser and compare bytes post-parse.
Fields marked expectedHash: 'SAME_AS_receipt_all_optional_absent' assert equality with the baseline vector; the test harness does not need a literal value, only the invariant.

Fields marked mustContainSubstring assert a substring appears in the canonical output — useful when the full canonical is verbose but one specific byte sequence is the thing being tested (e.g., that Go does not HTML-escape < and >).

Vectors marked preNormalizationInput: true carry schema-invalid caller input on purpose — they test the SDK's pre-serialization null→absent sweep. Do NOT attempt to schema-validate these `receipt` payloads; canonicalise and hash them and assert the invariant.

The number vectors rely on language-native IEEE 754 double-precision arithmetic. Each SDK should construct the test value from its numeric literal exactly as written in the input field; some JSON parsers may not round-trip -0 through JSON.parse, so implementations should hard-code -0 when the vector name is number_negative_zero rather than parsing it from this file.

The legacy 0.2.0 terminalChain in cross-sdk-tests/v020_vectors.json stays as-is (signature-preservation proof). New vectors added to v020_vectors.json (e.g. parametersDisclosureReceipt) and to this file use schemaVersion 0.2.1.
